I've also updated the 'Infrastructure Architecture' description to emphasize its backend-first design and simulation capabilities. I want to highlight my use of AI to accelerate the exploration of scheduling logic, edge cases, and heuristics.

I want to update the full screen content:
SchedIQ
Deterministic Resource Planning & Task Scheduling Engine
Why I Built SchedIQ

SchedIQ started from a very familiar frustration.

In fast-paced product environments, teams work across multiple modules, individuals juggle overlapping responsibilities, and roadmaps are constantly reshaped by user feedback and high-priority requests. On paper, plans look solid. In reality, priorities shift, capacity gets stretched, and dependencies surface too late.

As a project and delivery lead, I kept seeing plans fail not because teams couldn’t execute—but because the planning systems allowed optimistic commitments without validating feasibility.

SchedIQ is my attempt to fix that.
Not with better reporting, but by forcing reality into the planning conversation before commitments are made.

The Reality SchedIQ Is Designed For

SchedIQ is built for environments where:

Engineers contribute to multiple modules simultaneously

Ownership is distributed across teams, not isolated

High-priority requests interrupt planned work regularly

Reprioritization is constant and unavoidable

Traditional tools assume stability. Real product teams don’t have it.

SchedIQ treats every new request—planned or reactive—as a feasibility check. It evaluates impact against real capacity, ownership boundaries, and existing commitments, making trade-offs explicit instead of hidden behind urgency.

What SchedIQ Is (and Is Not)

SchedIQ is not a task tracker.

It doesn’t ask what you want to do.
It helps you understand what you can do—realistically and explainably.

At its core, SchedIQ answers three questions:

Can this task be delivered by the requested date?

If yes, what does the execution plan look like?

If no, what exactly is blocking it, and what needs to change?

Every answer is grounded in deterministic logic, not assumptions.

How It Works
1. Planning Without Commitment

Tasks are first created in a planning state.

At this stage:

No capacity is consumed

No assignments are locked

Scenarios can be explored safely

This separation is intentional. Planning should be cheap. Commitment should be deliberate.

2. Deterministic Feasibility Analysis

When analyzed, SchedIQ simulates execution by:

Respecting real working hours and weekends

Considering only already committed work

Enforcing ownership rules (primary and secondary owners)

Honoring cross-team and cross-module dependencies

The engine explores valid execution paths and determines whether a feasible schedule exists.

3. Explainable Outcomes

If a task is feasible, SchedIQ returns:

A realistic execution window

A clear delivery date

The ownership path used

If it’s not feasible, it explains:

Which team or individual is blocking it

Which task is causing the conflict

The exact time window of contention

What should change—reprioritization or timeline

This turns planning discussions from opinions into facts.

4. Explicit Commitment

Only after confirmation does a task move to committed.

At that point:

Assignments become capacity-blocking

Future plans respect this commitment

Delivery discipline is enforced automatically

Core Design Principles

Determinism over optimism
If a system allows impossible plans, people will make them.

Explanation over abstraction
A “no” is only useful if it clearly explains why.

Ownership matters
Work should flow to people who actually own the system.

Planning is not execution
Mixing the two is how silent overcommitment happens.

My Role

SchedIQ is something I designed and built end-to-end.

That includes:

Framing the problem from real delivery experience

Designing ownership, dependency, and capacity models

Implementing scheduling and feasibility logic

Defining strict lifecycle and state rules

Building the backend decision engine and APIs

This wasn’t built as a demo—it was built as a thinking tool I genuinely wished I had while planning real projects.

How I Used AI While Building SchedIQ

I don’t come from a traditional coding background, and I didn’t build SchedIQ by writing everything from scratch line by line.

Instead, I used AI as a force multiplier—to translate system thinking into working software.

AI helped me:

Turn product and delivery problems into concrete system designs

Explore scheduling logic, edge cases, and failure scenarios quickly

Generate and iterate on backend code based on clearly defined rules

Validate assumptions by simulating “what breaks if this changes?”

What mattered most wasn’t syntax—it was clarity of thought.

I focused on defining:

The right constraints

The correct ownership and lifecycle rules

Deterministic behavior that could be explained and trusted

AI handled much of the mechanical implementation. I owned the logic, decisions, and trade-offs.

SchedIQ works not because the code is clever, but because the rules are explicit, the constraints are real, and the system refuses to lie about feasibility.

Tech Stack

Next.js (App Router)

Supabase

PostgreSQL

Backend-driven decision logic

API-first architecture

The system is intentionally backend-heavy. Decisions are made server-side so the UI reflects reality, not optimism.

What SchedIQ Taught Me

Most delivery failures aren’t execution failures.
They’re planning failures that nobody surfaced early enough.

SchedIQ reinforced a belief I already had:
Good planning isn’t about confidence. It’s about constraints.

What’s Next

SchedIQ is still evolving. Areas I’m actively exploring:

Smarter capacity slot detection

Skill-based owner selection

Scenario simulation (“what if we move this?”)

Better parallel execution heuristics

But the core idea won’t change:

Commitments should be earned, not assumed.

**Updating SchedIQ Spec**

I've been meticulously refining the "Technical Spec" document for SchedIQ. My focus now is on content organization. I'm crafting distinct headings: "Why I Built SchedIQ," "Reality," "What SchedIQ Is/IsNot," "How It Works," "Principles," "My Role," "AI usage," and "Tech Stack." This structure aims for clarity and impact.


**Outlining SchedIQ's Future**

The SchedIQ Spec is evolving. I've begun to flesh out the document's structure, adding detail to each section. I'm focusing on "The Engine" section to clarify SchedIQ's functionality. This entails elaborating on the 4-step process and making sure the "AI as Force Multiplier" section truly highlights the development workflow. Additionally, I’m building out the "Leadership" section to fully explain my role.


**Incorporating Visuals & Details**

I'm making progress, adding visuals and detail to the SchedIQ Spec. Screenshots with captions are now integrated, and I'm updating `Projects.tsx` for modal content. Typographic refinements are underway, and the "Screenshots" align with the new narrative. I'm focusing on the component's structure: a sticky header, two-column layout, and full-width sections for clarity, while also building in the dark/light transition classes.


**Refining the UI's Layout**

I'm now prioritizing the UI's scrollable, well-structured layout to ensure readability. I'm adding icons for each subsection to maintain a high-end aesthetic. I have confirmed that the "Core Infrastructure" line has been removed, the SchedIQ logo slot is retained, and the modal content is complete. I'm also ensuring the provided text aligns with the modal's contents.

SchedIQ

Deterministic Resource Planning & Task Scheduling Engine

Why I Built SchedIQ

SchedIQ started from a very familiar frustration.

In fast-paced product environments, teams work across multiple modules, individuals juggle overlapping responsibilities, and roadmaps are constantly reshaped by user feedback and high-priority requests. On paper, plans look solid. In reality, priorities shift, capacity gets stretched, and dependencies surface too late.

As a project and delivery lead, I kept seeing plans fail not because teams couldn’t execute—but because the planning systems allowed optimistic commitments without validating feasibility.

SchedIQ is my attempt to fix that. Not with better reporting, but by forcing reality into the planning conversation before commitments are made.

The Reality SchedIQ Is Designed For

Built for environments where:

payload.text:
I want to add another project under Side quests.

🟦 SUMMARY CARD — Day Spent Well
Day Spent Well

A reflective time-awareness application

Strategic Objective

I built Day Spent Well to answer a simple but uncomfortable question: where does my day actually go?
The goal wasn’t productivity or optimization—but honest awareness by comparing planned time with reality, without guilt or pressure.

Capability Chips

Planned vs Actual Time Awareness

Second-Level Time Tracking

Insight-Driven Reports

Calendar-Based History

Infrastructure Architecture

A calm, mobile-first time-awareness system that separates tracking, aggregation, and insights. Time is captured precisely, aggregated at the data layer, and surfaced through reflection-focused dashboards rather than productivity metrics.

Architecture Tags

Time Aggregation Logic

Read-Only Historical Data

Insight Modeling

State-Driven UI

Operational Value

Helps users see how their days are actually lived

Encourages reflection instead of guilt

Makes lifestyle balance visible over time

Removes pressure-driven productivity patterns

Tech / Tools

React · TypeScript · Tailwind · Supabase · Vercel

Footer Tagline

Awareness before optimization
Designed to be opened even on a “bad” day.

🟦 FULL-SCREEN VIEW — Day Spent Well
A Reflective Time-Tracking & Insight Application
Why I Built Day Spent Well

Day Spent Well started from a personal realization.

I could plan my days carefully—work, health, learning, rest—but at the end of the week, I still couldn’t answer a basic question honestly: how did I actually spend my time? Most tools either pushed me to optimize harder or reduced everything to streaks and scores.

What I wanted instead was clarity—without guilt.

Day Spent Well is my attempt to build a tool that helps people see their time clearly before trying to change it.

The Reality It’s Designed For

Modern life doesn’t follow perfect schedules.

Days rarely go as planned

Energy fluctuates

Priorities shift

Some days are simply messy

Most productivity tools treat this as failure.
Day Spent Well treats it as reality.

The product is designed to support reflection across imperfect days, not to enforce discipline through pressure.

What Day Spent Well Is (and Is Not)

Day Spent Well is not a productivity or habit-tracking app.

It doesn’t:

Push streaks

Score your behavior

Judge “good” or “bad” days

It is a time-awareness system that helps users understand:

What they planned to do

What they actually did

How patterns emerge over days and weeks

How It Works
1. Daily Time Tracking

Users plan activities across common life categories:

Work

Health

Sleep

Essentials

Leisure

Learning

Activities are tracked using a start / stop timer with:

Pause and resume support

Seamless switching between activities

Second-level accuracy

Day-wise aggregation

2. Planned vs Actual Awareness

Each activity includes a planned duration.

The dashboard shows:

Actual time spent

Progress against plan

Remaining or unaccounted time

The design intentionally avoids language that implies failure. The comparison exists only to create awareness.

3. Calendar-Based History

Users can navigate past days through a calendar view.

Historical days are read-only

Empty days clearly indicate no tracking

Data integrity is preserved by design

This prevents rewriting history and encourages honest reflection.

4. Standardized Activity Categories

All activities are grouped into standardized categories that drive:

Visual identity (colors & icons)

Reporting logic

Data aggregation

The system cleanly separates:

Raw time data

Aggregated insights

UI presentation

5. Insightful Reports (Core Differentiator)

Reports are designed to feel reflective, not evaluative.

Supported report ranges:

Last 7 days

This week (Mon–Sun)

Metrics include:

Planned vs actual averages per category

Over-spent and under-spent categories

Consistency (High / Medium / Low)

Balance index (Stable / Slightly Skewed / Skewed)

Average time accounted per day

There are no scores, no grades, and no streak pressure.

6. Profile Management

Simple profile management includes:

Name, age, profession

Centralized menu for profile, reports, and sign-out

Clean, Apple-inspired navigation patterns

Design Philosophy

Day Spent Well is inspired by Apple Fitness and Apple Health, not productivity apps.

Design principles:

Calm, dark UI

Color used only to indicate state

No aggressive notifications

No motivational language

The app is intentionally designed to feel safe to open—even on difficult days.

My Role

I designed and built Day Spent Well end-to-end.

This included:

Product concept and philosophy

Information architecture

Time-tracking and aggregation logic

Insight modeling

UI and interaction design

Technical implementation

This project was built as a personal experiment grounded in real daily behavior—not as a growth-driven product.

How I Used AI While Building This

I used AI as a thinking and acceleration tool, not as a decision-maker.

AI helped me:

Translate product intent into working components

Iterate quickly on state logic and aggregation rules

Explore edge cases in time tracking and reporting

Move faster without compromising clarity

The design philosophy, rules, and constraints were always human-led. AI helped reduce friction, not define direction.

Tech Stack

Frontend: React (Vite), TypeScript

Styling: Tailwind CSS

Backend & Auth: Supabase

State Management: React hooks & derived state

Deployment: Vercel

Architecture Highlights

Clear separation of tracking, aggregation, and presentation

Time aggregation handled at the data layer

Read-only historical views to preserve integrity

Scalable foundation for weekly and monthly insights

What This Project Taught Me

People don’t need more productivity pressure.

They need clarity, context, and compassion for how time is actually spent.

Day Spent Well reinforced a belief I carry across all my work:
awareness is the most sustainable form of change.

What’s Next

Planned enhancements:

Weekly and monthly visual summaries

Gentle narrative insights

Long-term lifestyle balance trends

Optional end-of-day reflection prompts

Always with the same constraint:
no guilt, no pressure.

One-Line Pitch

Day Spent Well is a reflective time-awareness application that helps users understand how their days are actually lived—by comparing planned and real time without productivity pressure.

To make your "Side Quests" section scalable and more visually engaging, I will transition from a list-based layout to a **high-fidelity Interactive Project Studio**. 

### The Specification:
1.  **Project Grid System**: Instead of stacking massive cards, projects will be housed in a **responsive 2-column grid** (on desktop) with a sleek, compact summary state.
2.  **Visual "Studio" Aesthetic**: Each card will feature a refined "Work-in-Progress" vibe with blueprint-style backgrounds, tech chips, and status indicators (e.g., "Active Lab").
3.  **Unified Modal System**: A robust, single modal component that dynamically loads content based on which project is clicked. This prevents code bloat while allowing for infinitely detailed "Spec Sheets."
4.  **Color Identity**: Each project will have its own primary accent (Blue for SchedIQ, Emerald for Day Well) that ripples through its respective modal for clear visual branding.
5.  **Interactive Elements**: Hover effects will reveal "Operational Insights," making the cards feel like interactive dossier entries rather than static text blocks.

payload.text:
You are ONLY allowed to ADD ONE NEW PROJECT under the “Side Quests” section.

❌ Do NOT modify:

Any existing project (Enterprise or Side Quests)

Layout, styling, UI components, spacing, or visuals

Page structure, navigation, or global content

Existing tags, metrics, or text

❌ Do NOT edit or refactor existing content.

✅ You MAY ONLY:

Add a new Side Quest project

Populate its Summary Card content

Populate its Full-Screen / Detail View content

Treat this as literal content insertion only.
No summarization, no optimization, no creative rewriting.

🆕 NEW SIDE QUEST PROJECT
Project Name

Day Spent Well

Subtitle

A reflective time-awareness application

🟦 SUMMARY CARD CONTENT (USE EXACTLY)

Strategic Objective

I built Day Spent Well to answer a simple but uncomfortable question: where does my day actually go?
The goal wasn’t productivity or optimization—but honest awareness by comparing planned time with reality, without guilt or pressure.

Capability Chips

Planned vs Actual Time Awareness

Second-Level Time Tracking

Insight-Driven Reports

Calendar-Based History

Infrastructure Architecture

A calm, mobile-first time-awareness system that separates tracking, aggregation, and insights. Time is captured precisely, aggregated at the data layer, and surfaced through reflection-focused dashboards rather than productivity metrics.

Architecture Tags

Time Aggregation Logic

Read-Only Historical Data

Insight Modeling

State-Driven UI

Operational Value

Helps users see how their days are actually lived

Encourages reflection instead of guilt

Makes lifestyle balance visible over time

Removes pressure-driven productivity patterns

Tech / Tools

React · TypeScript · Tailwind · Supabase · Vercel

Footer Tagline

Awareness before optimization.
Designed to be opened even on a “bad” day.

🟦 FULL-SCREEN / DETAIL VIEW CONTENT (USE EXACTLY)
Day Spent Well

A Reflective Time-Tracking & Insight Application

Why I Built Day Spent Well

Day Spent Well started from a personal realization.

I could plan my days carefully—work, health, learning, rest—but at the end of the week, I still couldn’t answer a basic question honestly: how did I actually spend my time? Most tools either pushed me to optimize harder or reduced everything to streaks and scores.

What I wanted instead was clarity—without guilt.

Day Spent Well is my attempt to build a tool that helps people see their time clearly before trying to change it.

The Reality It’s Designed For

Modern life doesn’t follow perfect schedules.

Days rarely go as planned

Energy fluctuates

Priorities shift

Some days are simply messy

Most productivity tools treat this as failure.
Day Spent Well treats it as reality.

What Day Spent Well Is (and Is Not)

Day Spent Well is not a productivity or habit-tracking app.

It doesn’t push streaks, score behavior, or judge good or bad days.

It is a time-awareness system that helps users understand:

What they planned to do

What they actually did

How patterns emerge over days and weeks

How It Works

Daily Time Tracking

Users plan activities across common life categories like Work, Health, Sleep, Learning, Leisure, and Essentials. Activities are tracked using a start/stop timer with pause, resume, and seamless switching, offering second-level accuracy and clean day-wise aggregation.

Planned vs Actual Awareness

Each activity has a planned duration. The dashboard shows actual time spent, progress against plan, and remaining or unaccounted time—designed to inform, not pressure.

Calendar-Based History

Past days are accessible via a calendar view. Historical days are read-only to preserve data integrity, and empty days clearly indicate no tracking.

Insightful Reports

Reports focus on reflection rather than gamification. They include planned vs actual averages, over- and under-spent categories, consistency levels, balance indicators, and average time accounted per day.

Design Philosophy

Inspired by Apple Fitness and Apple Health, the interface is calm and dark, uses color only to indicate state, avoids aggressive notifications, and is designed to feel safe to open even on difficult days.

My Role

I designed and built Day Spent Well end-to-end, including product concept, information architecture, time-tracking logic, insight modeling, UI design, and technical implementation.

How I Used AI

I used AI as an acceleration and exploration tool—to translate intent into components, iterate on aggregation logic, and move faster without compromising clarity. The philosophy and constraints remained human-led.

Tech Stack

React (Vite) · TypeScript · Tailwind CSS · Supabase · Vercel

What This Project Taught Me

People don’t need more productivity pressure.
They need clarity, context, and compassion for how time is actually spent.

What’s Next

Planned enhancements include weekly and monthly summaries, gentle narrative insights, long-term balance trends, and optional end-of-day reflections—always without guilt or pressure.

❗ FINAL VALIDATION

If any existing project, layout, or content outside Side Quests is modified, immediately revert changes and apply ONLY the addition above.

Confirm completion without making any further edits.
